perturbation.original_text: 1. The Information will be kept confidential and shall not without your prior written consent, be disclosed by us, or by our agents, representatives or employees, in any manner whatsover, in whole or in part, and shall not be used by us, our agents, representatives or employees, other than in connection with the transaction described above.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. The Information will be kept confidential and shall not, except when deemed commercially reasonable, be disclosed by us, or by our agents, representatives or employees, in any manner whatsoever, in whole or in part, and shall not be used by us, our agents, representatives or employees, other than in connection with the transaction described above.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'except when deemed commercially reasonable' introduces ambiguity and subjectivity to the confidentiality clause. This could be interpreted in many ways, potentially allowing for disclosure under vaguely defined circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: The PUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By introducing the 'commercially reasonable' exception, the contract weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by PUTSA. The ambiguous language creates uncertainty regarding the actual level of protection afforded to the information, especially as it can be interpreted to be not compliant with trade secret protection laws.

perturbation.justification: The added phrase 'except when deemed commercially reasonable' introduces a subjective criterion that weakens the original confidentiality obligation. While the provided law snippets are not directly relevant, the Law Explanation states that the PUTSA (presumably the Protecting Trade Secrets Act) requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. The added phrase allows for disclosure based on a 'commercially reasonable' assessment, which could potentially fall short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by PUTSA, thus creating ambiguity and potentially violating the requirement to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Moreover, we agree to reveal the Information only to our agents, representatives and employees who need to know the Information for the purpose of evaluating the transaction described above, who are informed by us of the confidential nature of the Information and who shall agree in writing to act in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Moreover, we agree to make a good faith effort to reveal the Information only to our agents, representatives and employees who need to know the Information for the purpose of evaluating the transaction described above, who are generally informed by us of the confidential nature of the Information, and who will generally agree to act in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agree to reveal' with 'make a good faith effort to reveal', 'are informed' with 'are generally informed', and 'shall agree' with 'will generally agree' weakens the obligation to ensure confidentiality. This creates a loophole where failing to fully inform or obtain explicit agreement is acceptable as long as a 'good faith effort' is made.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The modified language, with its emphasis on 'good faith effort' and 'generally informed', fails to meet the standard of reasonable measures because it doesn't ensure that all individuals with access to trade secrets are explicitly aware of the obligations, thereby diminishing the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022) - Damages :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages. --Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages. --If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The modified contract language weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially violating PUTSA's requirement for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The original text ensures that agents, representatives, and employees 'shall agree in writing' and are 'informed' of the confidential nature. The modified text replaces these requirements with 'good faith effort,' 'generally informed,' and 'will generally agree,' introducing ambiguity. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 references damages for misappropriation in 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304, implying a legal expectation for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, which is undermined by the weakened obligations in the changed text. The law explanation supports the determination by stating that the modified language with terms such as 'good faith effort' fails to meet the standard of reasonable measures.

perturbation.original_text: 8. In the event that we or anyone to whom we transmit the Information pursuant to this Agreement becomes legally compelled to disclose any of the Information, we will provide you with prompt written notice and oral notice so that you may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. In the event that we or anyone to whom we transmit the Information pursuant to this Agreement becomes legally compelled to disclose any of the Information, we will attempt to provide you with written or oral notice so that you may seek a protective order or other remedy.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'we will provide you with prompt written notice and oral notice' to 'we will attempt to provide you with written or oral notice' weakens the obligation to notify the disclosing party. Removing 'appropriate' before remedy also creates some level of ambiguity as to what qualifies as a remedy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45(d)(3) outlines protection for trade secrets and confidential commercial information in the context of subpoenas. While this agreement isn't directly about subpoenas, the principle of providing notice when legally compelled to disclose information is vital for allowing the original owner to protect their trade secrets. Weakening the notice requirement hinders the trade secret owner's ability to seek protective measures under FRCP 45(d)(3) or similar legal principles, thus conflicting with the spirit and intent of the rule to protect confidential information during discovery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The change from 'we will provide you with prompt written notice and oral notice' to 'we will attempt to provide you with written or oral notice' significantly weakens the obligation to provide notice, creating ambiguity about whether any notice will actually be given. This undermines the ability of the original owner of the information to protect their interests, conflicting with the principle of protecting confidential information, which is vital for allowing the original owner to protect their trade secrets as described in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets do not directly address this specific obligation, the Law Explanation suggests that weakening the notice requirement hinders the trade secret owner's ability to seek protective measures, suggesting a violation of the intent to protect confidential information during discovery.

perturbation.original_text: 11. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. This Agreement shall be generally interpreted in a manner consistent with legal principles of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws' to 'shall be generally interpreted in a manner consistent with legal principles' introduces ambiguity. This phrase doesn't bind the interpretation strictly to Pennsylvania law, opening the door for arguments that deviate from established legal precedent within the state.
perturbation.law_citation: J.K. Harris & Co., LLC v. Dawson, 155 A.3d 652 (Pa. Super. 2017)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Pennsylvania law, contracts are to be interpreted to give effect to the intent of the parties, as evidenced by the language of the agreement. The modified clause weakens the link to Pennsylvania law by stating 'generally interpreted,' creating a space for arguments that are not rooted in Pennsylvania law, which weakens and possibly contradicts the standard for contract interpretation as established by Pennsylvania case law. J.K. Harris & Co., LLC v. Dawson is relevant because it highlights the importance of interpreting contracts according to established legal principles within the Commonwealth.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Google Scholar Loading... The system can't perform the operation now. Try again later. Settings Sign in Sign in Google Scholar 404. That's an error. Sorry, no content found for this URL That's all we know.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the phrase 'shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws' with 'shall be generally interpreted in a manner consistent with legal principles.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens the binding nature of Pennsylvania law. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that this change 'creates a space for arguments that are not rooted in Pennsylvania law, which weakens and possibly contradicts the standard for contract interpretation.' While the scraped snippets are either missing or unavailable, the Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, and a comparison of the original and changed text, leads to the conclusion that the modification introduces a significant enough ambiguity to contradict established legal principles, and so introduces a contradiction.
